coredata.dc:title: A Cu2O-CDs-Cu three component catalyst for boosting oxidase-like activity with hot electrons
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fu
Li, Ning
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Huiqi
Chang, Qing
Liu, Hantao
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: More challenges are faced for the design of an ideal multi-component photocatalyst because it is required to attain synergies of each component function. In order to boost light-driven chemical conversions, here we provides a Cu2O-CDs (carbon dots)-Cu three component oxidase-like catalyst that can effectively yield chemically useful energetic electrons under visible light irradiation. This catalyst shows light-boosting oxidase-like activity for catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and remarkably high reaction rate. By Michaelis–Menten model fitting, the obtained maximum reaction rate reaches to 9.47 μM min−1, which is much larger than other analogous nanozymes reported previously. Moreover, we carefully demonstrated the roles of each component played in light-boosting oxidase-like catalytic reaction and offered an insight into the mechanism of OPD oxidation in the presence of Cu2O-CDs-Cu. This work could open a promising route to ideal catalysts that can effectively couples thermal and photonic stimuli to drive oxidase-like activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hot carrier
Heterogeneous catalysis
Cuprous oxide
Carbon dots
Plasmonic nanostructure
O-phenylenediamine oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the reaction rate of OPD oxidation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125 , 51272301 , 51502270 , 21703209 ), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, and North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM image of Cu2O sample; (d) TEM image of Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (e) and (f) Further magnification images of the positions marked with red circle and purple rectangle in (d), respectively; (g) HRTEM image of Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (h) Line-scan profiles of Cu, O and C elements recorded along the red line shown in high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM image (Top); (i) Elemental mappings showing the distribution of Cu, O and C elements (green: Cu; red: O; purple: C; scale bar: 1.0 μm).

(a) Absorption spectra of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu, with inset their photos; (b) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of OPD solution in the presence of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu over 10 min visible light irradiation; (c) The plots of nonlinear fitting of Michaelis-Menten curves for OPD oxidation catalyzed by Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu under visible light irradiation and dark conditions; (d) The dependence of conversion rate from Cu2O-CDs-Cu on light intensity.

(a) EIS Nyquist plots of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (b) Conversion rate of OPD and QE from Cu2O-CDs-Cu as a function of incident wavelength; (c) and (d) The comparison of catalytic activities of Cu2O, Cu2O-Cu, Cu2O-CDs-Cu and CDs with and without light irradiation, respectively.

(a) Absorption spectra of NBT solution and (b) PL spectra of TA solution over Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu after light irradiation; (c) Comparison of conversion efficiency of Cu2O-CDs-Cu before and after adding h+,
O2 − and
OH radical scavengers, respectively; (d) XPS valence spectrum of Cu2O; (e) XPS valence spectrum Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (f) MS plots of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu; (g) Schematic illustration of the band structures; (h) Temperature effects on catalytic activities of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu.
Mechanism illustration of catalytic oxidation of OPD: (a) Catalytic process of Cu2O without illumination; (b) Catalytic process of Cu2O-CDs-Cu with illumination.

(a) The recyclability of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu for catalytic oxidation OPD at the same condition; (b) The corresponding XRD patterns of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu before used and after repeatedly used five times.
Comparisons of Km and Vmax parameters from different catalysts for OPD as substrate.

head.ce:title: A Cu2O-CDs-Cu three component catalyst for boosting oxidase-like activity with hot electrons

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: More challenges are faced for the design of an ideal multi-component photocatalyst because it is required to attain synergies of each component function. In order to boost light-driven chemical conversions, here we provides a Cu2O-CDs (carbon dots)-Cu three component oxidase-like catalyst that can effectively yield chemically useful energetic electrons under visible light irradiation. This catalyst shows light-boosting oxidase-like activity for catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and remarkably high reaction rate. By Michaelis–Menten model fitting, the obtained maximum reaction rate reaches to 9.47 μM min−1, which is much larger than other analogous nanozymes reported previously. Moreover, we carefully demonstrated the roles of each component played in light-boosting oxidase-like catalytic reaction and offered an insight into the mechanism of OPD oxidation in the presence of Cu2O-CDs-Cu. This work could open a promising route to ideal catalysts that can effectively couples thermal and photonic stimuli to drive oxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Plasmonics as energy input is a sustainable approach to boost light-driven chemical conversions [1]. To control over light and photochemistry, however, plasmonics requires the help of metallic nanostructures capable of concentrating light into nanoscale volumes [1–4]. This can lead to the production of intense electric fields, highly energetic (“hot”) electrons and holes. The hot electrons relax by locally heating the nanostructures [3,5,6]. All these phenomena can be utilized to efficiently manipulate, enhance, and drive chemical transformations [3]. Comparing with semiconductor photocatalysis, hot-carrier-driven transformations on plasmonic metal surfaces contribute to activation of the rate-limiting reaction and show a positive dependence on light intensity for conversion rates [6–8]. Moreover, the recently observed plasmon-induced interfacial charge-transfer transition (PICTT) allows for the interaction of surface plasmons with adsorbates on the surface of catalyst by transferring hot electrons to the specific unoccupied adsorbate orbital [9,10]. This offers a foundation to design plasmonic catalyst that can selectively promote favorable chemical pathways through engineering the interface of plasmonic nanoparticle and semiconductor [2].

Cuprous oxide (Cu2O) with narrow band gap of 1.9–2.4 eV is an excellent light-harvesting semiconductor as well as low cost and environmental compatibility [11–17]. However, it is not an ideal photocatalyst because of severe photocorrosion and poor capability of carrier separation and migration [11,18]. To tackling these issues, the driving forces to accelerate separation and migration of photogenerated electrons and holes are generally provided with the help of secondary components [11,19]. Many reports have demonstrated that carbon dots (CDs) combined with semiconductor photocatalysts can enable photogenerated carriers to separate and migrate rapidly due to their excellent carrier transfer ability [20–23]. Additionally, Li et al. have found the ability of the Cu2O-CDs composite to capture near infrared (NIR) and IR light for enhancing the amount of photogenerated carriers [24]. Accordingly, we chose the Cu2O-CDs complex as a support of plasmonic nanoparticles for highly efficient plasmonic photocatalysis. On the other hand, CDs also serve as reductants for the production of plasmonic Cu nanoparticles. Cu nanoparticles are particularly attractive because of Cu’s strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect in a broad spectral range, high natural abundance and low cost in contrast with noble metals, but their use is restricted by their inherent instability under atmospheric conditions [25,26]. Carbon nanostructures have exhibited the ability to stabilize Cu and Cu2O nanoparticles by altering their sensitivity to oxygen and/or water [24,27].

In this contribution, we have developed an approach toward in-situ reaction mediation components of catalyst, which endows the strong interactions between components and helps to create a desired interface electronic state appropriate to specific chemical transformation [9,28–30]. A three component catalyst of Cu2O-CDs-Cu was produced and showed an intrinsic oxidase-like activity, as it has an ability to quickly oxidize organic substrates without any peroxide agent (e.g·H2O2). This signifies that Cu2O-CDs-Cu can act as mimicking-enzymes (i.e. nanozyme) and is promising to replace specific enzymes in enzyme-based applications. In particular, its activity can be enhanced remarkably by visible light irradiation. Using the catalytic oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to form phenazine-2,3-diamine (called OPDox) as the model reaction (which is of great importance in immunoassay determination of enzyme-catalyzed reactions such as oxygenase/oxidases and peroxidases) [13,31,32], the Cu2O-CDs-Cu displayed maximum reaction rates of OPD to OPDox from 2.07 to 9.47 μM min−1 when illuminated with visible light. Our observed positive dependence of photocatalytic reaction rate on light intensity indicated that Cu2O-CDs-Cu could effectively couple thermal and photonic stimuli to drive oxidation reaction. The hot electrons, formed through the excitation of surface plasmons on illuminated Cu nanoparticles, enhance the overall reaction rate through an electron-assisted O2 − dissociation process and an exclusive activation for H+ desorption.

All chemicals such as copper (II) acetylacetonate (Cu(acac)2), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), formic acid (88 v/v%), H2O2 (30 v/v%), NaBH4, and NaSO4 were purchased from Aladdin, and they were directly used without further purification in our experiments. All aqueous solutions in the experiment were prepared using ultrapure water.

CDs were prepared based on our previous report CDs [33]. Typically, 200 mg of coal pitch powder was added into a mixed solution including 30 mL of formic acid (88 v/v%) and 3 mL of H2O2 (30 v/v%), and the solution was stirred for 20 h at room temperature. Then, the solution after the reaction was centrifugated for 30 min at 10000 rpm, and the supernatant liquid containing the suspension of CDs was retained, and the precipitate was discarded. Finally, the supernatant liquid was evaporated to get CDs powder.

In a 50mml beaker, 80 mg Cu(acac)2, 20 mg CDs, and 6 mL H2O were added into 24 mL DMF, followed by 30 min stirring. The homogeneous black solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor and heated from room to 160 °C and kept for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the bottom of teflon lining would have precipitates. The collected precipitates were centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and acetone, respectively, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The comparison sample of Cu2O was prepared in a similar manner, but there are no CDs.

TEM and HRTEM were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of different samples were obtained using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE powder diffractometer at Cu Kα with a scanning rate of 2° min−1 in the 2θ range of 5–90°. Light absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of samples were recorded as KBr pellets with a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 500 cm−1. XPS data of all samples were collected using a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode electrochemical cell system. Working electrodes were prepared by coating ethanol suspensions to the samples onto indium tin oxide (ITO) glass electrodes, which were then dried in a vacuum at 200 °C for 2 h to evaporate the solvent. 0.5 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte solution, and a Platinum plate and Ag/AgCl reference electrode acted as the counter electrode and reference electrode. Linear potential scans were performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 within a given potential window. All of the data were collected on a Bio-Logic (SP 120) electrochemical workstation.

5 mg of solid sample powder was added in 15 mL an aqueous solution of OPD. The solution was irradiated with the light intensity 50 mW/cm2. Phenazine-2,3-diamine (OPDox) was the catalytic oxidation product of OPD, and the characteristic absorption of OPDox is at 417 nm (ε = 16300 M−1cm−1), which was monitored using UV–vis spectrophotometry by taking aliquots at regular intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the reaction rate of OPD oxidation
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration of OPDox (C) can be obtained using the Lambert beer's law:
A = ε × b × C
where A is absorbance which can be measured; ε is the molar extinction coefficient of OPDox, (ε = 16300 M−1cm−1); b is the path length of light (b = 1 cm).

The reaction rates were fitted by Michaelis-Menten equation:
v =
V
m a x
×
[ S ]
(
S
+
K m
)
where V max is the maximal reaction velocity; K m is the Michaelis constant; [S] is concentration of reaction substrate.

Characterization of as-produced two samples by X-ray diffraction (XRD) showed apparently difference in phases. The characteristic peaks at 29.6°, 36.4°, 42.3°, 61.4, 73.6 and 77.4° correspond to the (1 1 0), (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) planes of the Cu2O cubic phase (PDF# 78-2076), while three diffraction peaks located at 43.3°, 50.4° and 74.1° are assigned to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0) and (2 2 0) of the Cu cubic phase (PDF#85-1326), respectively (Fig. 1 a). By contrast, it can be found that metal Cu is formed accompanying with Cu2O phase in the presence of CDs. These results are also supported by Energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) analysis (Table S1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal different morphology features for two samples (Fig. 1b and d). Cu2O sample shows polyhedron shape (Fig. 1b) and its high resolution TEM images (HRTEM) taken near the particle edge reveals distinct lattice fringes with a planar spacing of 2.4 nm, corresponding to the (1 1 1) lattice planes of Cu2O [12] (Fig. 1c). Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite is spherical shapes with a core-shell structure (Fig. 1d). Further magnification images for the marked region manifest that the shell is composed of small nanoparticles (Fig. 1e and f). HRTEM image reveals distinct interface between crystalline grains in small particles (Fig. 1g). By EDS line scan analysis, it can be observed that Cu and C elements reveal different distributions along the red line, whereas few O elements exist (Fig. 1h). The C elements mainly distribute in the domains labeled by elliptical shapes where less Cu elements appear. Accordingly, CDs could play a key role in reduction of Cu2O to metal Cu. Moreover, the anisotropic distribution of Cu, C, and O was demonstrated by elemental mapping of Cu2O-CDs-Cu. From Fig. 1i, Cu and O elements are distributed across the volume of the particle while C atoms primarily occupy its surface. Further characterizations of infrared spectroscopy (IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were also performed for confirming the formation of Cu2O-CDs-Cu (Figs. S1). By comparisons, almost all of typically absorbance peaks of the O-containing stretching vibration functional groups (e.g.-COOH, -OH) observed in the IR spectrum of CDs either strongly decrease or disappear entirely in the IR spectrum of Cu2O-CDs-Cu (Fig. S1a). On the other hand, Cu0, Cu2O and CuO bonds are also presented in XPS Cu 2p3/2 and O1s spectra, respectively (Fig. S1b–d). These results imply that CDs were successfully combined with Cu2O and Cu phases.

Diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu with the same amount are compared in Fig. 2 a. The Cu2O sample exhibits pink color and a broad absorption covering the entire visible region. The Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite shows dark red color and much stronger light absorption in the whole wavelength testing region comparing with merely Cu2O. However, no obvious LSPR absorption induced by Cu nanoparticles is observed in the spectrum of Cu2O-CDs-Cu. To confirm it, we also collected the absorption spectra of CD powders and Cu2O-Cu (Fig. S2a), which was fabricated by directly reducing Cu2O using NaBH4 (XRD pattern of Cu2O-Cu shown in Fig. S2b, Supplementary data). By contrast, the Cu2O-Cu sample displays a very similar light absorption characteristic with merely Cu2O. According to previous reports [25,34], the LSPR absorption of Cu can be broadened and red shifted with oxide formation and its intensity decreases sharply. Therefore, no obvious LSPR absorption in the presence of Cu nanoparticles could be attributed to the effect of Cu2O. Moreover, the Tauc plot of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu are shown in Fig. S2c and then obtained their band gap energies are around 1.94 and 2.05 eV, respectively. These values are in well accordance with the results measured by the linear potential scans (See the following description and supplementary data). Therefore, the Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite has a larger band gap than Cu2O.

Nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities attract much attention due to their ability to replace specific enzymes in enzyme-based applications [35]. A few nanomaterials including of CDs, Fe3O4, V2O5, Au nanoparticles, have been reported to mimic the activity of peroxidase, which in principle catalyze the oxidation of substrates in the presence of peroxides (mostly H2O2) [36–39]. Differently, here we made the novel discovery that as-prepared Cu2O-CDs-Cu sample possesses light-boosting oxidase-like activity. Natural oxidases catalyze the oxidation of substrates using O2 as the electron acceptor and subsequently O2 is reduced to water or H2O2 (Scheme S1a) [35]. Taking catalytic oxidation of OPD (Scheme S1b) in the absence of H2O2 as an example [13,31], we estimated the effects of Cu and CDs on oxidase-like activity and the reaction rate. OPD is a key precursor for preparing numerous heterocyclic compounds with unique properties and its oxidation reaction has been widely used for evaluating the catalytic activity of the multi-copper containing oxidase (Laccase) [32]. The new absorption peak at around 417 nm appears as a result of the formation of OPDox [13,31]. What’s more, the OPDox solution emits an orange-yellow fluorescence on the excitation of its own absorption band. Thus the production of OPDox can be visualized by tracking the gradual increase in the absorption intensity of typical peak at 417 nm. The overall oxidation reaction and kinetics can be easily monitored by using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Hence, UV–vis spectroscopy has become an highly efficient and reliable means to measure reaction rates of OPD oxidation [13,31,32]. Fig. 2b shows the absorption of OPD solution in the presence of as-prepared Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu without adding any peroxides over 10 min visible light irradiation, respectively, and their corresponding photoluminescence (PL) spectra excited at 417 nm. By contrast, it can be found that the absorption and PL intensities resulted from Cu2O-CDs-Cu are considerably higher than those of Cu2O. The catalytic conversion of OPD to OPDox for Cu2O-CDs-Cu is more than three times the amount of Cu2O based on absorption data. This indicates that Cu2O-CDs-Cu has better catalytic oxidation performance than single Cu2O under visible light irradiation.

Functional nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking characteristics are generally called as nanozymes. Their catalytic reaction kinetics shall follow the Michaelis-Menten equation widely used to describe the activities of natural enzymes [13,35]. In consequence, the oxidase-like activities of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu for OPD oxidation were investigated based on Michaelis-Menten theory. Under the same mass of catalysts, the changes of reaction rates were examined with OPD concentrations in reaction solution (Fig. S3). According to the resultant data, we obtained an ideal hyperbola relationship that follows the classic Michiaelis-Menten equation through nonlinear Hill function fitting [13,32]. From Fig. 2c, it can be observed that the catalytic reaction rate of Cu2O-CDs-Cu is much higher than that of Cu2O at variable OPD concentrations under visible light irradiation. From these plots, we calculated the kinetic parameters of apparent Michaelis–Menten constant (Km ) and maximum reaction velocity (V max). As shown in Table 1 , the apparent Km of OPD oxidation is 1.69 and 3.94 mM while the V max is 2.07 and 9.47 μM min−1 for Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu irradiated by visible light, respectively. Actually, these apparent Km values are lower than those of Cu@Cu2O aerogels (8.88 mM), NiFe2O4 (8.4 mM) and ZnFe2O4 (22.6 mM), indicating that our presented samples have better binding affinity for OPD. Surprisingly, the V max values of Cu2O-CDs-Cu irradiated by visible light are 6.7-fold, 18.2-fold and 19.3-fold higher than those of Cu@Cu2O aerogels, NiFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4 as mimic peroxidases, respectively [13,32]. Hence, our presented Cu2O-CDs-Cu possesses much higher activity for catalytic oxidation OPD.

In addition, the kinetic plot of Cu2O-CDs-Cu on the dark condition and the corresponding Km and Vmax parameters were provided in Fig. 2c and Table 1, respectively. It can be seen that the Cu2O-CDs-Cu on the dark condition exhibits poor binding affinity and lower catalytic activity for OPD oxidation. All of above data clearly reveal that the visible light irradiation remarkably promotes catalytic conversion of OPD to OPDox. To further demonstrate this, the effects of light intensities on catalytic activities of samples were also investigated and shown in Fig. 2d. Under the same time of irradiation, the absorption intensity of characteristic peak at around 417 nm increases with the visible light source intensity (Fig. S4 provides its emission spectrum). This accounts for more OPDox production in reaction solution with light intensity enhancement. At the meantime, the reaction rate of OPD oxidation employing Cu2O-CDs-Cu as the photocatalyst increases with the light intensity yet. Almost a linear dependence of the reaction rate on the source intensity is observed. This demonstrates that the catalytic reaction activity of Cu2O-CDs-Cu for OPD oxidation has significant positive correlation with the number of absorbed photons.

To verify the roles of CDs in promoting photogenerated carrier transfer, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique was employed to study interfacial carrier transfer performance [11,23]. The carrier transfer resistance is generally reflected by the radius of the semicircle in a Nyquist plot at high frequency. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the carrier transfer resistance of Cu2O-CDs-Cu is distinctly smaller than that of Cu2O. Thereby, CDs are beneficial to accelerate carrier transfer across the solid–liquid interface. This reason could give rise to higher catalytic activities of Cu2O-CDs-Cu than Cu2O under visible light irradiation. Besides small interfacial carrier transfer resistance, photoinduced oxidation of OPD with the help of Cu2O-CDs-Cu could have an electronic origin. In an attempt to testify it, we conducted the study of the light-assisted conversion rate as a function of optical filter wavelength. Fig. 3b shows the wavelength dependence of conversion amount for a fixed light intensity at 50 mW cm−2. Moreover, quantum efficiency (QE) was calculated as the conversion rate divided by the rate of photons impinging on the catalyst bed for various wavelengths, and also shown in Fig. 3b. A turning point at 600 nm filter appears from both the conversion amount and QE with light wavelengths. At present, it has been demonstrated that the extinction induced by the LSPR of Cu nanoparticles starts from 565 nm and their LSPR extinction peak can be broadened and red shifted with oxide formation [25,34], whereas the extinction intensity decreases sharply. This phenomenon could be also explained to why the LSPR signature of metallic Cu nanoparticles is not so obvious in absorption spectrum of Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite, but it exhibits better photocatalytic activities than single Cu2O.

To confirm the roles of CDs in Cu2O-CDs-Cu, we examined catalytic activities of CDs and the sample of Cu2O-Cu for oxidation of OPD to OPDox with and without light irradiation, respectively. Under visible light irradiation, the catalytic activities of CDs and Cu2O-Cu for OPD are in the middle of that of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu at the same condition (Fig. 3c). Nevertheless, CDs show lower activities than Cu2O-Cu. With no illumination, it can be found that not only the activity of Cu2O-Cu is far inferior to that of Cu2O sample, but also CDs almost entirely lose their catalytic activity for OPD oxidation (Fig. 3d). This result illustrates that the oxidation activity of CDs alone originates from photogenerated carriers. Both CDs and Cu nanoparticles combined with Cu2O synergistically boost oxidase-like catalytic reaction of OPD oxidation on Cu2O. Besides reducing carrier transfer resistance, LSPR-induced hot carriers from Cu nanoparticles could be also a key factor. The detailed processes of photoinduced carriers participating in catalytic oxidation reaction need to be investigated.

To explore further the underlying mechanism that governs the light-induced conversion rate enhancement, we detected reactive species, which are considered to be involved in the actual oxidative and reductive reactions in catalysis caused by photoinduced electrons (e−) and holes (h+) generated on the heterogeneous solid surfaces of photocatalysts [40]. As a result of the reduction by
O2 −, nitro blue tetrazorium (NBT, −0.05 V) becomes formazan and hence it has been utilized to detect
O2 − in photocatalysis [41]. After irradiating for 5 min, NBT alkaline solution added Cu2O-CDs-Cu shows larger degrees of absorption decrease in characteristic peak than that of Cu2O (Fig. 4 a). This indicates that Cu2O-CDs-Cu can generate more amount of
O2 − than Cu2O. Since terephthalic acid (TA) reacts with
OH to generate 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) that can emit unique fluorescence at around 426 nm [23], similarly we probed the amount of
OH generated from Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu as the photocatalysts, respectively; and found that Cu2O-CDs-Cu also produced more amount of
OH than Cu2O at the same conditions (Fig. 4b). In order to verify the role of these reactive species, the ascorbic acid (VC) and isopropanol (IPA) were further used as
O2 − and
OH radical scavengers during catalytic oxidation OPD process, respectively (Fig. S5) [42]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the conversion amount of OPDox in the presence of VC decreases significantly, while the addition of IPA has little effect on the oxidase-like activity of Cu2O-CDs-Cu under visible light irradiation. When adding Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) as h+ scavenger [43], the oxidase-like activity of Cu2O-CDs-Cu is also greatly inhibited. Obviously, photoinduced h+ and
O2 − accelerate the conversion of OPD to OPDox rather than
OH.

The valence band (VB) positions of as-prepared Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite were ascertained by the XPS valence spectra and the results are shown in Fig. 4d and e. Their VB values are determined as 1.54, and 1.16 V (vs. NHE), respectively. The conduction band (CB) positions are further estimated from their VB and band gap values, i.e. −0.4 and −0.89 V (vs. NHE) for Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu, respectively. To verify these results, linear potential scans are further used to analyze VB and CB positions. Their values are +1.54 and −0.4 V vs. NHE for Cu2O and +1.16 and −0.89 V vs. NHE for Cu2O-CDs-Cu (Fig. S6), and thus the band gap energies determined from these potential scans were 1.94 and 2.05 eV for Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu, respectively. These data are in well agreement with those mentioned above. The change of energy level positions relative to Cu2O is attributed to the incorporation of CDs, which could be similar with the roles of cluster distortions introduced into the crystal lattice for electronic structure adjustment [23,44]. The similar case has also been observed in nanodiamond-embedded Cu2O nanostructure. That is, the VB and CB positions of Cu2O phase moved upward as nanodiamonds were embedded in its nanostructure [11]. Besides this, some works have demonstrated that the band structures of the Cu2O-based heterostructures can be tuned with other nanophases, such as pure and doped ZnO [14].

The type of conductivity and Fermi levels of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu were determined by Mott–Schottky (MS) plots. From Fig. 4f, all of their plots show a negative slope, which is typical for n-type semiconductor [45]. Thus, the photogenerated electrons could serve as the main carriers in the presented samples under light irradiation. According to the intercepts of the extrapolated straight lines on the abscissa, the Fermi levels (Ef ) are −0.65 and −0.58 V (vs. NHE) for Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu, respectively. According to above data, the band structures are schematically illustrated in Fig. 4g. Comparing with the potentials of
OH/-OH and O2/
O2 −, it is confirmed that the photogenerated electrons can react with oxygen to form the oxidizing
O2 −, but the holes are difficult to react with H2O or −OH to yield oxidative
OH. Therefore, our detected
OH species in light-driven catalytic oxidation could originate from the following reaction [46]:
(2)
2
O 2
· -
+ 2
H
+
→ 2
HO
·

Carrier density N d was evaluated from the MS plot slopes by the following equation [11]:
(3)
N d
= 2
(
e 0
ε
ε 0
)
- 1
d (
C
- 2
) / d V
- 1
in which e 0 is the electron charge, ε and ε 0 is the dielectric constant and vacuum permittivity, respectively. The calculated N d of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu are 4.1 × 1022 and 3.0 × 1022 cm−3, respectively. The large carrier density of Cu2O could be attributed to its Fermi level with higher position than its CB. Since the collective oscillation of free carriers occurs at N d over 1021 cm−1 [47], the LSPR effect can be possibly evoked in our presented samples.

Because hot carriers created by LSPR can lead to appreciable local heating [3], their mediated chemical conversion is definitely influenced by thermal excitation [4]. Taking plasmon-mediated O2 dissociation on Ag nanostructure as an example, the catalytic reaction rate increased exponentially with temperature under white-light irradiation [48]. Thereby, we explored the effects of temperature on catalytic conversion of OPD to OPDox. Both samples reveal that the conversion amount of OPD to OPDox apparently increases with temperature (Fig. 4h). In contrast with Cu2O, the Cu2O-CDs-Cu displays higher temperature sensitivity. According to the results shown in Fig. 2c and Table 1, the Km values of Cu2O-CDs-Cu can be significantly influenced by visible light irradiation. The lower Km value on illumination implies that the transient local temperature induced by LSPR of Cu nanoparticles improves the affinity of Cu2O-CDs-Cu for OPD. This may be one reason of high reaction rate under light irradiation.

A possible mechanism for light-assisted catalytic oxidation of OPD via our presented Cu2O-CDs-Cu was illustrated in Fig. 5 . Cu2O nanophase has an intrinsic oxidase-like activity and so it can slowly oxidize OPD without hydrogen peroxide [35]. In details, the electrons can be transferred from a self-oxidation reaction of Cu2O (to form CuO) to oxygen and OPD molecules adsorbed on the surface of catalysts [18]. These electrons allow for the activation of NH bonds of OPD and subsequently incur nuclear motion along the NH2-dissociation reaction coordinate. Meanwhile, the releasing H species interact with CuO formed at the surface and leads to its return to Cu2O and the formation of H+ ions (Fig. 5a). The formed
O2 − radicals is reduced to H2O and H2O2 by reacting with H+ ions.

With illumination, the photogenerated electrons and holes should be generated and then directly involve in the oxidation reaction of OPD in absence of Cu nanoparticles (Fig. 5b). Based on EIS results (Fig. S7), CDs significantly increase separation and migration capability of photogenerated electrons and holes in Cu2O. As expected, more electrons migrated to the surface react with oxygen to form
O2 − radicals [40]. At the surface of catalysts, then these radicals could combine with H+ ions quickly to yield
OH as shown in Eq. (2) [46], promoting the process of OPD oxidation through desorption of H+ in time. On the other hand, the holes can accelerate self-oxidation reaction of Cu2O to give rise to much more electrons using for activation of NH bonds of OPD. As a result, the conversion rate of OPD to OPDox is enhanced with the help of illumination. Cu nanoparticles have low ability to capture H atoms and photoelectrons [49]. Thereby, excitation of Cu surface plasmons could accelerate the transfer of excited electrons to adsorbed O2 in the presence of Cu nanoparticles. The hot electrons are scattered into the antibonding orbital of adsorbed O2 and lead to stretched vibration of the O-O bonds [48,50]. As a result, this behavior facilitates the
O2 − dissociation step. The other effect of hot electrons is to push the vibration of superficial H+ ions over desorption activation barrier and then accelerate the reaction of H+ with
O2 −, boosting OPD transformation [4,51]. In addition, the hot holes can also activate reaction steps associated with OPD oxidation and desorption of the corresponding products which require vibrational energy in general [51]. The net effect of the photoinduced electrons and holes boosts oxidase-like activity of Cu2O for OPD oxidation while the LSPR effect of Cu nanoparticles is more relevant at kinetic regimes of oxidizing OPD. Therefore, Cu2O-CDs-Cu nanocomposite effectively couples thermal and photonic stimuli to drive OPD oxidation.

Because the delocalized electrons in carbon nanostructure can move freely in the sp2-bonded carbon atom network with a low resistance, the carbon vacancies or dangling bonds can stabilize chemical bonds of Cu atoms by influencing their electronic structure; thus the Cu2O and Cu nanoparticles coupled with carbon nanostructures cannot be oxidized to CuO [27]. To demonstrate this, we conducted stability tests of Cu2O and Cu2O-CDs-Cu catalysts in the reaction solution leading to OPD oxidation. The catalysts were recovered by simple centrifugation followed by washing with water/ethanol. Both samples suffer a little loss in activity during the cycles, but their stability becomes robust after the third catalytic reaction (Fig. 6 a). This phenomenon could result from two reasons. One may be that a small amount of samples cannot be recovered during the cycles due to their small sizes; the other may be relevant to their chemical stability. To clarify, further XRD characterizations were performed as shown in Fig. 6b. It can be found that no new peaks appear in XRD patterns of both samples before and after OPD oxidation, suggesting no CuO formation. XPS characterization further confirms the presence of both Cu0 and Cu+ in Cu2O-CDs-Cu after reused five times (Fig. S8). All of above results demonstrate that the catalytic stability of Cu2O-CDs-Cu is relatively high.

In conclusion, our work developed a novel Cu2O-CDs-Cu three component oxidase-like catalyst using for chemical transformation, and demonstrated the roles of each component played in light-assisted catalytic oxidation process through full characterizations and contrast experiments. Using catalytic oxidation of OPD to form OPDox as the model reaction, Cu2O-CDs-Cu exhibits high reaction rate and excellent oxidase-like activity because it can effectively yield chemically useful energetic electrons with illumination. We anticipate that our work opens up a new route to design ideal catalysts that can couple thermal energy and the energy of photons.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51502270, 21703209), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, and North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.122484.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
